does: This paper identifies and characterizes underspecification in language modeling tasks, where multiple valid outputs satisfy the training loss despite only one being selected. The authors develop a causal framework explaining how underspecification generates spurious correlations and introduce two lightweight black-box evaluation methods to detect inference-time task underspecification. Applied to gendered pronoun resolution across models ranging from BERT-base to GPT-4 Turbo, the work uncovers previously unreported spurious correlations between gender and temporal/spatial features. The evaluation is comprehensive, covering models with diverse pre-training objectives (masked, autoregressive, mixed) and training stages (pre-training through RLHF).
connects: This work extends prior research on dataset underspecification and its role in learning spurious correlations, connecting to broader literature on distributional shift and model biases. It builds on causal inference approaches to understanding machine learning failures and relates to prior studies on gender bias in language models, particularly pronoun resolution tasks. The paper responds to limitations in existing evaluation methods by proposing lightweight, task-agnostic approaches that don't require model internals. It also engages with work examining how different pre-training paradigms and training stages introduce or mitigate systematic biases, as demonstrated through comparisons across architectures and RLHF-trained systems.
enables: This work opens pathways for developing more robust evaluation frameworks that can generalize across model architectures and training procedures to detect task underspecification. The causal perspective on spurious correlations could inform better data collection practices and loss function design to reduce underspecification at training time. The methodology enables future work to systematically audit other structured prediction tasks for hidden spurious correlations, potentially improving fairness and robustness of deployed language models. Additionally, the findings suggest new training objectives or regularization techniques that could discourage learning spurious gender-temporal-spatial relationships while maintaining natural language quality.
